questions.question: Define a function f: A → B and explain the terms domain, codomain, and range with an example.
keyPoints: A function f from A to B assigns exactly one element of B to each element of A
Domain is set A of all possible inputs
Codomain is set B of possible outputs
Range is set of actual outputs produced by f
Example with specific sets and mapping

questions.question: Explain the difference between one-to-one (injective) and onto (surjective) functions with an example of each.
keyPoints: One-to-one: different inputs give different outputs
Onto: every element in codomain has at least one preimage
One-to-one example: f(x) = 2x from ℝ to ℝ
Onto example: f(x) = x³ from ℝ to ℝ
Key distinction: injectivity vs surjectivity properties

questions.question: State and explain the necessary and sufficient condition for a function to have an inverse.
keyPoints: Function must be bijective (one-to-one AND onto)
One-to-one ensures no two inputs map to same output
Onto ensures every codomain element has preimage
Only then can we define f⁻¹ such that f⁻¹(f(x)) = x
If not bijective, inverse is not a function

questions.question: Let f: ℤ → ℤ be defined by f(x) = 3x + 1. Prove that f is one-to-one but not onto.
keyPoints: To prove one-to-one: assume f(a) = f(b)
3a+1 = 3b+1 implies 3a = 3b implies a = b
To prove not onto: find element in codomain with no preimage
Let 2 be in codomain, solve 3x+1=2 gives x=1/3 not in ℤ
Thus 2 has no integer preimage, so not onto

questions.question: What is the composition of two functions? If f: A → B and g: B → C, define (g ∘ f) and explain its domain and codomain.
keyPoints: Composition (g ∘ f)(x) = g(f(x))
Apply f first, then apply g to the result
Domain of g ∘ f is domain of f (A)
Codomain of g ∘ f is codomain of g (C)
g ∘ f maps from A to C

questions.question: Explain the floor function ⌊x⌋ and ceiling function ⌈x⌉ with examples for positive and negative numbers.
keyPoints: Floor gives greatest integer ≤ x
Ceiling gives smallest integer ≥ x
Both are functions from ℝ to ℤ

questions.question: Prove that if f: A → B and g: B → C are both one-to-one, then (g ∘ f) is also one-to-one.
keyPoints: Assume (g ∘ f)(x₁) = (g ∘ f)(x₂)
This means g(f(x₁)) = g(f(x₂))
Since g is one-to-one, f(x₁) = f(x₂)
Since f is one-to-one, x₁ = x₂
Therefore (g ∘ f) is one-to-one

questions.question: Discuss the classification of functions with respect to being one-to-one and onto. For each type (injective, surjective, bijective), provide two examples from ℝ to ℝ and explain why they satisfy the property.
keyPoints: Define one-to-one: f(x₁)=f(x₂)⇒x₁=x₂
Define onto: for every y in codomain, exists x with f(x)=y
Injective example 1: f(x)=x³ (strictly increasing)
Injective example 2: f(x)=eˣ (strictly increasing)
Surjective example 1: f(x)=x³ (covers all ℝ)
Surjective example 2: f(x)=arctan(x) (range (-π/2,π/2))
Bijective example 1: f(x)=x³
Bijective example 2: f(x)=x (identity)
Explain why each satisfies the property
Mention counterexamples: x² is neither injective nor surjective on ℝ

questions.question: Let f: ℝ → ℝ be defined by f(x) = x². Answer the following: (i) Is f one-to-one on ℝ? Justify. (ii) Is f onto ℝ? Justify. (iii) What restriction on domain makes f one-to-one? (iv) What is the range of f? (v) Can f have an inverse? Explain.
keyPoints: (i) No: f(1)=f(-1)=1, different inputs map to same output
(ii) No: negative numbers in ℝ have no real square root preimages
(iii) Restrict domain to [0,∞) or (-∞,0] for one-to-one
(iv) Range is [0,∞) (all non-negative reals)
(v) No inverse on ℝ because not bijective
Could have inverse on restricted domain [0,∞) giving f⁻¹(y)=√y

questions.question: Prove that a function f: A → B is bijective if and only if there exists a function g: B → A such that (g ∘ f) = Iₐ and (f ∘ g) = Iᵦ, where Iₐ and Iᵦ are identity functions.
keyPoints: (⇒) If f is bijective, define g(y) = the unique x such that f(x)=y
Since f is onto, such x exists; since f is one-to-one, x is unique
Then g(f(x)) = g(y) = x = Iₐ(x)
And f(g(y)) = f(x) = y = Iᵦ(y)
(⇐) If such g exists, show f is one-to-one: f(x₁)=f(x₂)⇒g(f(x₁))=g(f(x₂))⇒x₁=x₂
Show f is onto: for any y∈B, let x=g(y), then f(x)=f(g(y))=y
Thus f is bijective with inverse g

questions.question: Consider the function f: ℕ → ℕ defined by f(n) = n². (i) Find the domain, codomain, and range. (ii) Is f one-to-one? Prove or disprove. (iii) Is f onto? Prove or disprove. (iv) If we change domain to ℝ, how do your answers change?
keyPoints: (i) Domain = ℕ, Codomain = ℕ, Range = {1,4,9,16,...} = set of perfect squares
(ii) Yes, one-to-one: if m²=n² then m=n (positive square roots)
(iii) Not onto: 2 in ℕ has no preimage (√2 not in ℕ)
(iv) On ℝ: still one-to-one on [0,∞), not one-to-one on full ℝ
On ℝ: still not onto ℝ (negative outputs)
Range on ℝ is [0,∞)
If domain restricted to [0,∞), f becomes bijective with inverse √x
